concepts.title: Metal Excess Defects
concepts.conceptBody:
Metal Excess Defects

It has been observed that if a crystal of $\text{NaCl}$ is heated in sodium vapour, it acquires a yellow colour. This yellow colour is due to the formation of a non - stoichiometric compound of sodium chloride in which there is a slight excess of sodium ions. What happens in this case is that some sodium metal gets doped into the sodium chloride crystal each atom of which gets ionised into $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ and ${{\text{e}}^{-}}$due to crystal energy. This electron occupies a site that would otherwise be filled by a chloride ion as in figure (a).

concepts.title: Metal Deficiency Defects
concepts.conceptBody:
Metal Deficiency Defects

In certain cases, one of the positive ions is missing from its lattice site and the extra negative charge is balanced by some nearby metal ion acquiring two charges instead of one. There is, evidently, a deficiency of the metal ions although the crystal as a whole is neutral. This type of defect is generally found amongst the compounds of transition metals which can exhibit variable valency as in figure (b).
